name: Basava
dates_notes: Dated from the Kalyāṇa-Cālukya court records (Basava served as chief minister and treasurer to the Kalacuri king Bijjala II at Kalyāṇa from c. 1156 to 1167) and from his own dated vacanas; modern Karnataka historical scholarship (Nandimath, Ishwaran, Ramanujan) accepts c. 1131–1167 CE.

dates_evidence.description: Kalyāṇa-Cālukya / Kalacuri court records of Basava's tenure as chief minister and treasurer to Bijjala II.
Vacanas with internal dating-references; Cennabasavapurāṇa as the principal Vīraśaiva hagiographic chronicle.

lineage_polemical.note: Madhva's Tattva-Saṅkhyāna and the anti-Vīraśaiva sections of the Tattva-Viveka engage the Vīraśaiva movement's iṣṭa-liṅga theology and its rejection of caste-bound Vedic ritual. The polemic is one of the few cases of a Vedāntic school engaging a Bhakti-tradition operating outside the classical darśana grid; Madhva treats Vīraśaiva as one of the contemporary nāstika positions to be answered.

Appayya Dīkṣita's Śivārka-Maṇi-Dīpikā engages the wider Śaiva tradition (Śaiva-Siddhānta and the Vīraśaiva movement) from a Smārta-Advaita-Śaiva position; the engagement with Vīraśaiva is partial but significant — Appayya treats the iṣṭa-liṅga theology as a popular-Śaiva variant whose metaphysical commitments deserve doctrinal scrutiny.

core_thesis: Basavaṇṇa is the founder of the twelfth-century Vīraśaiva movement in Karnataka. Born to a Brahmanical family, he became chief minister and treasurer to the Kalacuri king Bijjala II at Kalyāṇa, and from that position founded an *anubhava-maṇṭapa* ("hall of experience") at which Śaraṇas (devotees) of every caste, gender, and occupation gathered to compose *vacanas* (sayings — short oral-poetic prose-poems in Old-Kannada) and to articulate a Śaiva devotional theology built around three distinctive commitments. First, *iṣṭa-liṅga* worship: each devotee receives at *dīkṣā* a personal *liṅga* (worn on a string around the neck) and worships *only* this *liṅga* throughout life — replacing temple-Brahmanical Śiva-worship with internal-personal Śiva-worship and dissolving the priestly intermediary. Second, the *aṣṭāvaraṇa* ("eight-fold protection"): *guru*, *liṅga*, *jaṅgama* (the wandering renunciate as living Śiva), *vibhūti* (sacred ash), *rudrākṣa*, *mantra* (the *Śiva-pañcākṣarī*: *namaḥ śivāya*), *pāda-tīrtha* (foot-water of the *guru* / *jaṅgama*), and *prasāda* (consecrated food). Third, the radical anti-caste, anti-gender-hierarchy, anti-ritual-Brahmanism social vision worked out in the *vacanas* — the *kāyaka* doctrine that all honest labor is sacred, the *dāsoha* doctrine that the wealth one earns belongs not to oneself but to the community of Śaraṇas. Basava's *vacanas* are in Old-Kannada (not Sanskrit), and the morphological analysis of his work follows Old-Kannada (Halegannada) grammar rather than Pāṇinian Sanskrit grammar.

engaged_works.title: Basava Vacanas
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composed orally during Basava's Kalyāṇa period; subsequently collected into vacana-saṅgrahas by the Vīraśaiva tradition.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: The Vīraśaiva tradition ascribes a corpus of vacanas to Basava (the figures vary — Nandimath's standard reference catalogs c. 1500–2500 vacanas under Basava's signature-line 'kūḍala-saṅgama-deva'); modern critical scholarship (Ishwaran, Ramanujan, Sreenivasamurthy) treats some as Basava's own compositions and others as later ascriptions in his style. The signature-line — each vacana ending with the address kūḍala-saṅgama-deva ('Lord of the meeting rivers') — is the standard authentication-marker. Wave-10: A.K. Ramanujan Speaking of Śiva (Penguin 1973) text-PDF acquired; Kannada-with-English translation, text-extractable.

ascription_evidence.description: Vīraśaiva tradition's authentication via the kūḍala-saṅgama-deva signature; Cennabasavapurāṇa as principal hagiographic source.

Nandimath (A Handbook of Vīraśaivism); Ishwaran (Religion and Society Among the Lingāyats); Ramanujan (Speaking of Śiva, the principal English-language anthology); Sreenivasamurthy (Karnataka university editions).

engaged_works.summary: An oral-poetic corpus of short prose-poems in Old-Kannada (Halegannada), each ending with the signature-address *kūḍala-saṅgama-deva* ("Lord of the meeting rivers" — a Śiva-form Basava had worshipped at Kūḍala-saṅgama in his youth). The vacanas are not metrical verse but prose-poetry built on parallelism, repetition, and rhythmic close. Their themes cover the entire Vīraśaiva doctrinal-disciplinary frame: the *iṣṭa-liṅga* worship, the *aṣṭāvaraṇa*, the *pañcācāra* (the five disciplines: *liṅgācāra*, *sadācāra*, *śivācāra*, *bhṛtyācāra*, *gaṇācāra*), the *kāyaka* doctrine that all honest labor is sacred, the *dāsoha* doctrine that wealth belongs to the community of Śaraṇas, the *anubhava-maṇṭapa*'s anti-caste anti-gender-hierarchy ethical vision. The polemical edge — directed against caste-Brahmanism, temple-priestly intermediation, and Vedic-ritualist exclusion of non-twice-born from the Śaiva path — is sharp throughout: 'making the river that goes by your house holy / making the trees in your yard sacred / how about the worship in the heart?' (a representative Basava-style vacana on internal worship versus external Brahmanical purity). The vacana-corpus is the principal source for Vīraśaiva theology and the form in which the Vīraśaiva movement enters the textual record. A.K. Ramanujan's *Speaking of Śiva* (1973) is the standard English-language anthology and remains the principal point of access for non-Kannada readers. Note on language: the morphological analysis of these texts follows Old-Kannada (Halegannada) grammar (verbal forms in *-uvenu*, *-uttide*, etc.; the agglutinative case-system of Kannada with eight cases including the *prathamā / dvitīyā / tṛtīyā* paradigm in the Kannada inflectional pattern) rather than Pāṇinian Sanskrit grammar.

reception_notes: Basava is the founder of the Vīraśaiva movement, which becomes one of the major Bhakti-traditions of the South Indian medieval period and is engaged by later Vedāntic and Śaiva scholars (Madhva, Appayya, the Smārta tradition of the early-modern period). The movement's anti-caste, anti-Vedic-ritual social vision exerts significant pressure on the South Indian Brahmanical tradition. The Cennabasavapurāṇa (thirteenth century, attributed to Cennabasava's school) is the principal hagiographic chronicle. Modern scholarship — Nandimath's A Handbook of Vīraśaivism, Ishwaran's Religion and Society Among the Lingāyats, Ramanujan's Speaking of Śiva — has established Basava as a major figure in the comparative study of medieval Indian Bhakti traditions and as a figure whose theological commitments deserve consideration in the analysis of how Vedāntic engagement extends beyond the classical darśana grid into Bhakti-traditions operating in vernacular registers.
